1: כֻּלָּךְ יָפָה — this phrase appears in v7 as the full statement; in v1 the description builds toward it. מִבַּעַד לְצַמָּתֵךְ (miba'ad letzammatekh) — 'behind your veil' — tzammah is usually rendered 'veil' or 'locks of hair'; the image is either of the eyes visible through the veil or the eyes framed by the hair. The flock of goats descending from Gilead (hehar hagil'ad) — Gilead was the trans-Jordanian highland known for its goat herds; the comparison of flowing dark hair to a flock descending the hillside is a visual metaphor for movement, abundance, and dark color.
2: כְּעֵדֶר הַקְּצוּבוֹת (ke'eder haqetzuvot) — 'like a flock of freshly shorn sheep' — the teeth compared to freshly washed sheep emerge from the water clean, bright, and perfectly matched (shekulam taamot — all matched in pairs). שַׁכֻּלָה (shokulah) — 'bereaved/missing one' — from the root for childlessness or bereavement; the point is that not one tooth is missing, just as no ewe is without its twin. The unusual vocabulary of grief (shokulan) in a beauty description creates a poignant contrast: nothing is lost, nothing is missing.
3: חוּט הַשָּׁנִי (chut hashani) — 'thread of scarlet' — the scarlet thread (shani) appears in the Tabernacle curtains (Exodus 25:4) and in Rahab's scarlet cord (Joshua 2:18) — a sign of protection and covenant mark. The beloved's lips are like this covenant-marker thread. מִדְבָּרֵךְ (midbarekh) — 'your mouth' — literally 'your speech/word'; the same root as midbar (wilderness) but also meaning word, speech. The beloved's speech is lovely (naveh).
4: מִגְדַּל דָּוִיד (migdal David) — 'the tower of David' — the specific identification of the architectural reference. The tower built in courses (talpiyot — possibly a technical term for courses of stone, or a place name) with a thousand shields hung on it — all the shields of the warriors. The neck like a tower bearing shields is a striking metaphor: the beloved's neck carries the weapons that protect — the strength and dignity of her bearing becomes a military image of both beauty and power.
5: שְׁנֵי שָׁדַיִךְ (shnei shadayikh) — 'your two breasts' — shad (breast) also relates to the divine name El Shaddai (God Almighty), connected by some scholars to the root shad (to nourish, to give milk). The comparison to twin gazelle fawns (te'ome tzevi — twins of a gazelle) feeding among the lilies suggests nourishing abundance, matched proportion, and delicate beauty simultaneously.
6: הַר הַמֹּר (har hamor) — 'the mountain of myrrh' — a unique geographic reference; it may be the Temple Mount (associated with myrrh in the incense), a metaphorical description of the beloved's body, or an actual hill. גִּבְעַת הַלְּבוֹנָה (givat halevonah) — 'the hill of frankincense' — the two most sacred incense ingredients of the Temple offering (Exodus 30:34). The beloved's body is compared to the mountain of sacred incense — the place where heaven and earth meet in the fragrance of worship.
7: כֻּלָּךְ יָפָה רַעְיָתִי (kullakh yafah ra'yati) — 'you are altogether beautiful, my love' — kullakh (all of you, entirely you) is the emphatic totality; every part just described is summed in the whole-person declaration. וּמוּם אֵין בָּךְ (umum ein bakh) — 'and there is no defect in you' — mum is a technical term from the Levitical purity code for the blemishes that disqualify animals for sacrifice and priests for service (Leviticus 21:17-23; Song of Solomon 22:17-25). The declaration of no blemish uses the priestly vocabulary of sacrificial perfection: the beloved is entirely suitable, without disqualifying mark.
8: אִתִּי מִלְּבָנוֹן כַּלָּה (itti millevanon kallah) — 'with me from Lebanon, O bride' — the first use of kallah (bride) in the Song; it appears six times in Song of Solomon 4-5 as the primary address for the beloved. Kallah means both 'bride' and 'daughter-in-law' (from kalal, to complete, to perfect). The invitation to come down from Lebanon — from the mountain peaks of Amana, Senir, and Hermon (the northern frontier) — is an invitation from the wild heights to the intimate home.
9: לִבַּבְתִּנִי (libbavtini) — 'you have captivated/stolen my heart' — a denominative verb from lev (heart), occurring only here in the Hebrew Bible. It is a newly coined verb meaning 'you have given me a heart attack' or 'you have taken/captured my heart.' The Piel stem (libbavtini) implies the beloved has actively done this to the lover, not accidentally. The doubling in v9 — 'with one of your eyes, with one jewel of your necklace' — emphasizes that a single glance has the full effect; she does not need to deploy all her beauty.
10: מַה יָּפוּ דֹדַיִךְ (mah yafu dodayikh) — 'how beautiful is your love' — the echo of the opening verse (Song of Solomon 1:2: your love is better than wine) is explicit; the beloved now mirrors the language back. מֵיַּיִן (meyayin) — 'than wine' — the same comparison as 1:2 but now the beloved speaks it to the woman, not the woman speaking it of the man. The reciprocity of the Song's language is intentional: the same terms of love apply equally to both.
11: נֹפֶת תִּטֹּפְנָה שִׂפְתוֹתַיִךְ (nofet titofnah siftotayikh) — 'your lips drip with nectar' — nofet (nectar, honeycomb drippings) is the finest form of honey, the free-dripping liquid from the comb. תַּחַת לְשׁוֹנֵךְ (tachat leshonekh) — 'under your tongue' — honey and milk under the tongue; the physical location of the sweetest taste. The fragrance of her garments compared to Lebanon connects to the cedar forests whose scent was proverbially fresh and pure.
12: גַּן נָעוּל (gan na'ul) — 'a locked garden' — the triple description: locked garden, sealed spring, closed fountain all speak of enclosure and exclusivity. The participle na'ul (locked, bolted) uses the same root as the bolt on a door (Nehemiah 3:3). In the ANE, the enclosed garden was both a symbol of paradise (the Persian pairideaza = walled garden = paradise) and of the protected woman in marriage. The locked garden is not imprisonment but exclusive dedication: she is a garden that belongs entirely to one.
13: שְׁלָחַיִךְ (shelachayikh) — 'your branches/shoots' — from shalach (to send out, extend); the beloved's body is described as an orchard with extending branches. The catalogue of spices and fruits — pomegranates (rimmonim), henna (kopher), nard (nerd) — parallels the incense-spice lists of Exodus 30 and the merchant-spice inventories of the ancient Near East. The woman's body is a garden of the most precious aromatic commodities of the ancient trade routes.
14: The extended spice list continues: nard and saffron (karkom), calamus (qaneh) and cinnamon (qinnamon), frankincense trees (atzei levonah), myrrh (mor) and aloes (ahalot) — the precise vocabulary of the incense trade. Several of these plants (calamus, cinnamon, cassia) appear in the holy anointing oil formula of Exodus 30:23-25. The beloved is simultaneously a personal garden and a catalogue of the sacred. The MKT's 'all the finest spices' captures the superlative quality.
15: מַעְיַן גַּנִּים (ma'yan gannim) — 'a spring/fountain of gardens' — the same term as v12's sealed spring, now opened and flowing. The imagery shifts from enclosure (locked, sealed) to outpouring (fountain, streams). נֹזְלִים מִן לְבָנוֹן (nozlim min levanon) — 'streams flowing from Lebanon' — the melting snows of Lebanon fed the Jordan and the coastal rivers; fresh water from the mountains was the purest and most life-giving. The beloved as a mountain spring connects to the wilderness imagery (Song of Solomon 3:6: coming up from the wilderness) and the living water of prophetic promise (Isaiah 58:11).
16: עוּרִי צָפוֹן וּבוֹאִי תֵימָן (uri tzafon uvoi teiman) — 'awake, O north wind; come, O south wind' — the beloved summons the two winds to blow through her garden. The north and south winds bracket the entire compass; she calls the full range of natural forces to carry her fragrance to her beloved. יָבֹא דוֹדִי לְגַנּוֹ (yavo dodi legano) — 'let my beloved come to his garden' — she has already made the shift from 'my garden' to 'his garden'; the gift of herself precedes the union of Song of Solomon 5:1.
